title: AP: DHS Granted $20M for Body Cameras; ICE Agents in Fatal Houston Shooting Had None
excerpt: An Associated Press investigation found that despite Congress allocating $20 million for ICE body cameras and repeated DHS pledges to deploy them, the agents who fatally shot Lorenzo Salgado Araujo in Houston had not been issued cameras — nearly six months after the same promise was made following the January Minneapolis shootings.
content:
An Associated Press report published July 10, 2026, documented the gap between DHS promises and body camera reality: Congress had allocated $20 million in April for the "procurement, deployment and operations" of body cameras for immigration enforcement officers, yet agents involved in the July 7 Houston shooting had none.

As of March 2026, then-acting ICE Director Todd Lyons informed Congress that only about 3,000 of the agency's 13,000 agents — fewer than one in four — were equipped with body cameras, even though a February 2025 internal directive had mandated their use during enforcement activities. A government shutdown had delayed contract awards, and the Trump administration had initially proposed cutting the body-camera program before Congress restored it.

  • DHS claimed body cameras had been "deployed to more than half the field offices," with the remainder to receive them within 60 days.
  • Former DHS Secretary Kristi Noem had pledged a "rapid" deployment after the January 2026 Minneapolis shootings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti — a promise still unfulfilled months later.
  • Sen. Angus King noted the absence of cameras: "They've got tons of money. Why they don't have body cams I think is a very fair question."
mediaPlaceholder: AP investigation documents showing gap between DHS body camera funding and actual deployment to field agents
